// Module
var code = "<h1 id=setting-up-a-development-environment>Setting up a Development Environment</h1> <p>In order to build the website for testing on your own machine, you’ll need to set up a <a href=https://nodejs.org>Node.js</a> development environment. The site targets the LTS version of Node (currently major version 12).</p> <p>Building the site requires the following external dependencies:</p> <ul> <li>Node.js</li> <li>Yarn</li> <li>Pandoc, v2.8 or higher. Make sure it’s available on your <code>PATH</code>.</li> </ul> <h2 id=external-dependencies>External Dependencies</h2> <h3 id=windows>Windows</h3> <h4 id=node>Node</h4> <p>Download the latest 12.X release from <a href=https://nodejs.org>the Node.js website</a>. When installing, make sure to check “Automatically install the necessary tools” on the “Tools for Native Modules” screen – or follow the link to the manual instructions.</p> <figure> <img src=./tools-for-native-modules.png alt=\"\"/><figcaption>The “Tools for Native Modules” screen. Make sure the highlighted box is checked.</figcaption> </figure> <h4 id=yarn>Yarn</h4> <p>Once Node is installed, run</p> <div class=sourceCode id=cb1><pre class=\"sourceCode powershell\"><code class=\"sourceCode powershell\"><span id=cb1-1><a href=#cb1-1 aria-hidden=true></a>npm install -g yarn</span></code></pre></div> <p>from an administrator terminal.</p> <h4 id=pandoc>Pandoc</h4> <p>If you don’t already have it installed, and you used the “Automatically install the necessary tools” option when installing Node, you can easily install Pandoc by running</p> <div class=sourceCode id=cb2><pre class=\"sourceCode powershell\"><code class=\"sourceCode powershell\"><span id=cb2-1><a href=#cb2-1 aria-hidden=true></a>choco install -y pandoc</span></code></pre></div> <p>from an administrator terminal. If you don’t use <code>choco</code> to install Pandoc, make sure that the <code>pandoc</code> command is visible on your <code>PATH</code>.</p> <h3 id=linux>Linux</h3> <p>On Ubuntu, the easiest way to install Node is to use the snap package, which comes bundled with Yarn:</p> <div class=sourceCode id=cb3><pre class=\"sourceCode sh\"><code class=\"sourceCode bash\"><span id=cb3-1><a href=#cb3-1 aria-hidden=true></a><span class=ex>snap</span> install --classic node --channel=12</span></code></pre></div> <p>For other distros, download the <a href=https://nodejs.org/en/download/ >Linux binaries</a> or consult the <a href=https://nodejs.org/en/download/package-manager/ >instructions for installing via package manager</a>.</p> <h2 id=other-dependencies>Other Dependencies</h2> <p>Once node and yarn are installed, run</p> <pre><code>yarn install</code></pre> <p>To install the rest of the dependencies.</p> <h2 id=running-the-dev-server>Running the Dev Server</h2> <p>Once your development environment is set up, you can run the dev server, which provides nice things like automatic reloading when a file is changed. Run:</p> <pre><code>yarn start</code></pre> <p>then open a new tab pointing to <code>localhost:8080</code>. You’ll need to stop and restart the dev server if you make changes to the webpack configuration.</p> <h2 id=building-the-site>Building the Site</h2> <p>Run</p> <pre><code>yarn build</code></pre> <p>to build the production static site, output into the <code>dist</code> folder. You can run</p> <pre><code>yarn build-dev</code></pre> <p>if you need to build a static version of the development site.</p> ";
// Exports
module.exports = code;
